text.title: Golden Set Curation Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every golden-set curation must enforce. These prevent the most common failure: a golden set that looks healthy but doesn't catch real regressions.

rule.statement: Seed coverage across at least 4 buckets: happy_path, edge_case, adversarial, known_failure_class. Distribution target ≥ 15% in each bucket. Pure happy-path golden sets are rejected.
rule.rationale: Happy-path-only sets pass with 95%+ accuracy on broken models because the broken behavior only shows under load or weird inputs. Stratification forces the set to expose what production exposes.

rule.statement: Every golden item must carry an anti_output field: at least one plausible-wrong output that the model should NOT produce. Reject items with only expected_output.
rule.rationale: Anti-outputs catch "right answer, wrong reason" regressions and improve LLM-judge calibration. Without them, judges over-credit outputs that contain the right tokens for the wrong reason.

rule.statement: Every production incident involving the AI feature MUST produce at least one golden-set candidate within 7 days of the postmortem. Track conversion ratio: target ≥ 80% incidents → ≥ 1 candidate.
rule.rationale: Incidents are pre-labelled, free, high-value data points. Teams that skip incident-to-golden conversion repeat the same bug class quarterly.

rule.statement: Every 90 days, audit each golden item against current production behavior. Tag still_valid, schema_changed, retire. Retire items where the expected_output no longer matches updated business rules. Track retired-count per audit.
rule.rationale: Golden sets degrade ~ 10-20% per quarter through silent business-rule drift. Without retirement discipline, the set anchors the model to an outdated ground truth.

rule.statement: New items enter via PR with: dataset_version bump, per-item metadata complete, coverage report attached, peer review by ≥ 1 reviewer outside the proposing author. No direct push to main.
rule.rationale: Unversioned datasets cause silent regressions where the eval pipeline runs against a different set than CI expects. Reviewer requirement catches biased labels from the author.

example.title: Stratified golden item
example.code: id: gld-0042 feature: invoice-extraction bucket: known_failure_class # bucket for incident-derived items input: pdf_uri: golden/invoices/0042.pdf expected_output: vendor_name: "Acme GmbH" total_eur: 1234.56 tax_id: "DE123456789" anti_output: - vendor_name: "Acme Corp" # wrong country variant - tax_id: "VAT123456789" # wrong tax-id prefix metadata: difficulty: medium added_from: incident-2026-04-12 reviewer: jdoe@team.io
example.note: Illustrates r1 + r2 + r3: stratified bucket, anti_output present, incident provenance.

example.title: Happy-path-only seeding
example.code:
golden/items: - id: gld-0001 ... bucket: happy_path - id: gld-0002 ... bucket: happy_path - id: gld-0003 ... bucket: happy_path ... 47 items, all happy_path
Result: model that fails on every malformed input passes the golden eval with 100% accuracy. Incident-derived bugs never trigger regressions.
example.note: Illustrates r1 violation. Stratification is non-optional.
